title: Paths and project trees

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Paths and project trees; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Pro Git; second edition online; section applicable to Paths and project trees
sources.claim: Shell commands resolve relative paths from the current working directory, while absolute paths begin at a filesystem root; dot and dot-dot name current and parent locations.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Shell commands resolve relative paths from the current working directory, while absolute paths begin at a filesystem root; dot and dot-dot name current and parent locations.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: From repo/src, ../README.md resolves to the README in repo, while ./README.md looks inside src and may not exist. Your goal is to navigate and explain a nested project tree without relying on the current GUI location. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf tools files. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Shell commands resolve relative paths from the current working directory, while absolute paths begin at a filesystem root; dot and dot-dot name current and parent locations. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Windows and POSIX roots, separators, case rules, quoting, links, and permission behavior differ, so a path valid on one machine may not identify the same location elsewhere. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Every relative path starts from the repository root, regardless of the terminal's current working directory.

includes: Resolve absolute, relative, parent and home paths and account for spaces and case behavior.
Navigate and explain a nested project tree without relying on the current GUI location.
excludes: Windows and POSIX roots, separators, case rules, quoting, links, and permission behavior differ, so a path valid on one machine may not identify the same location elsewhere.

outcomes: Navigate and explain a nested project tree without relying on the current GUI location.

examples.title: Paths and project trees: direct case
examples.situation: From repo/src, ../README.md resolves to the README in repo, while ./README.md looks inside src and may not exist.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: From repo/src, ../README.md resolves to the README in repo, while ./README.md looks inside src and may not exist.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Inspect the working directory first, resolve each segment from that location, and quote paths as data according to the active shell's rules.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Inspect the working directory first, resolve each segment from that location, and quote paths as data according to the active shell's rules.

examples.title: Paths and project trees: boundary case
examples.situation: A directory name containing spaces is passed as one quoted argument instead of being split into several shell words.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A directory name containing spaces is passed as one quoted argument instead of being split into several shell words.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Windows and POSIX roots, separators, case rules, quoting, links, and permission behavior differ, so a path valid on one machine may not identify the same location elsewhere.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Every relative path starts from the repository root, regardless of the terminal's current working directory.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Navigate and explain a nested project tree without relying on the current GUI location.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every relative path starts from the repository root, regardless of the terminal's current working directory.
misconceptions.correction: Inspect the working directory first, resolve each segment from that location, and quote paths as data according to the active shell's rules.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Every relative path starts from the repository root, regardless of the terminal's current working directory.

analogy.example: A labeled filing room can model Paths and project trees: current location changes how relative directions resolve, and moving differs from copying a folder.
usefulFor: Reasoning about exact locations, inspection, file operations, and destructive scope
limitations: Shell expansion, permissions, links, filesystem rules, and command execution can behave unlike physical folders.
Windows and POSIX roots, separators, case rules, quoting, links, and permission behavior differ, so a path valid on one machine may not identify the same location elsewhere.
